purpose: Stages fiscal dominance progression from early warning to entrenched regime, providing an organizing heuristic for where a country stands on the spectrum.
components: Signal 1: Persistent primary deficits above structural threshold
Signal 2: r-g convergence and debt dynamics deterioration
Signal 3: Implicit Treasury-Fed coordination (inherently ambiguous)
Signal 4: Term premium reflecting fiscal supply rather than monetary policy
Signal 5: Inflation expectations de-anchoring and formal financial repression
current_assessment: US scores 2.0-2.5 of 5 signals as of early 2026, with Japan circa 2005-10 as closest analogue. Currently on the gradual path (<50bp/quarter term premium velocity).
limitations: Fitted to n=3-4 cases with zero degrees of freedom — definitionally over-fitted
Not falsifiable: no specified time horizon, no progression probability, no disconfirmation criteria
Signal 3 is inherently ambiguous and not operationally measurable
'2.5 of 5' should NOT be read as '50% of the way to fiscal dominance'
Appropriate for scenario organization, not probability estimation

purpose: Categorizes how fiscal dominance transmits through the financial system, with distinct asset class sequencing per channel, enabling scenario-specific portfolio construction.
components: Channel A — Financial Repression (~50% for US): gradual, negative real rates, wealth transfer from creditors to debtors over 10-20 years. Precedents: US 1942-51, France 1945-63, Japan 2016-present
Channel B — Acute Crisis (~25% for US): rapid term premium spike, market dislocation, forced policy response. Precedents: Italy 2011, UK 2022
Channel C — Monetization (<5% for US): explicit debt monetization, high/hyperinflation. Precedents: Argentina, Weimar
Residual (~20%): fiscal consolidation, productivity growth, or other resolution avoiding all three channels
current_assessment: The US is most likely on Channel A (financial repression / boring middle scenario), producing 3-6% inflation and persistently negative real bond returns. The basis trade is the key swing variable that could shift to Channel B.
limitations: Probability assignments (50/25/5/20) are ordinal rankings from n=7, not reliable cardinal numbers
Directional ranking (repression > acute > monetization) is well-supported; specific percentages are not
Does not specify transition conditions between channels
Each channel has its own internal sub-variants not fully explored

purpose: Provides observable, real-time market signals for tracking fiscal dominance progression and distinguishing gradual from acute pathways.
components: ACM term premium level (currently +50-100bp) and velocity (<50bp/quarter = gradual, >100bp/quarter = acute)
10Y auction bid-to-cover ratio (currently ~2.3-2.5x, declining)
Indirect bidder share at Treasury auctions (currently ~65%, down from 72%)
5Y5Y breakeven inflation (currently 2.3-2.5%, well below 3.0% trigger)
Stock-bond correlation bifurcation (2Y-SPX negative vs. 30Y-SPX positive)
Gold/copper ratio correlation regime (tentative, confidence 3/10)
current_assessment: All indicators are in the early/gradual zone. Term premium velocity is below the acute threshold. Auction metrics are deteriorating but not at crisis levels. Breakevens remain well-anchored. The correlation bifurcation is emerging.
limitations: Post-QE structural break makes pre-QE regression coefficients unreliable
QT-era sample (~3 years) is too short for reliable re-estimation
Specific threshold values (e.g., 150bp term premium) carry 4-5/10 confidence
Real-time correlation measurement is noisy
No single indicator is sufficient — requires composite assessment

purpose: Identifies the negatively skewed payoff profile where fiscal deficits simultaneously compress credit spreads (rewarding carry) and increase fiscal dominance risk (increasing tail risk).
components: Kalecki-Levy identity: deficits → corporate profits → credit spread compression
Fiscal dominance risk: same deficits → debt sustainability deterioration → tail risk
Payoff profile: carry narrows as underlying risk rises
Regime-conditional factor allocation: shift from carry-seeking to tail-hedging as fiscal dominance signals activate
current_assessment: The carry trap is currently operating — credit spreads are compressed while fiscal fundamentals deteriorate. The deficit is the common cause of both phenomena, creating a feedback loop that rewards complacency.
limitations: No precise threshold for when the trap becomes a measurably bad risk-reward
Requires regime identification, which is itself subject to the small-n problem
Harvey-Liu-Zhu framework shows fiscal dominance beta would fail t-stat threshold of ~3.0 in cross-sectional tests
Timing the regime shift is not possible with available tools

purpose: Classifies economies into gradual or acute fiscal dominance transition paths based on institutional absorption capacity, enabling scenario-specific risk assessment.
components: Gradual path features (10-25 years): reserve currency status, dollar-denominated debt, deep domestic capital markets, high domestic ownership of government debt, current account surplus
Acute path features (months): current account deficit, high foreign ownership of government debt, leveraged amplification mechanisms (basis trade), political fragmentation
No historical moderate-paced transitions observed (n=5)
Mechanism: institutional absorption capacity determines whether fiscal pressure accumulates slowly or breaks through suddenly
current_assessment: The US has a mix of gradual (reserve currency, deep markets) and acute (current account deficit ~3-3.5% GDP, ~30% foreign ownership, basis trade amplification) features. Currently on the gradual path per term premium velocity.
limitations: Based on n=5 historical cases — small sample
The US's mixed feature set has no direct precedent
Does not specify conditions for path-switching from gradual to acute
The basis trade as a path-switching mechanism is theorized but untested (n=0 for this specific scenario)

purpose: Estimates the probability of a self-reinforcing fiscal stress spiral by decomposing into required links, correcting for conjunction fallacy and positively correlated conditions.
components: Link A: Deficits remain large (high probability given political dynamics)
Link B: Term premium rises significantly (moderate probability, partially offset by maturity management)
Link C: Blended rate exceeds nominal GDP growth (mechanically tightening via r-g convergence)
Link D: No credible consolidation occurs (high probability given structural barriers)
Link E: Fed cannot intervene without inflation consequences (conditional on inflation regime)
Circuit-breakers: Treasury maturity management (30-50bp compression), political response to deficit salience, denominator inflation
Positive correlation adjustment: links are not independent, so joint probability exceeds independent product
current_assessment: Fair estimate: 20-30% over 5 years. Not 40-50% (naive reading of 6/10 confidence) nor 8-17% (challenger_02's independent-link calculation which underweights positive correlation). Basis trade amplification is the key swing variable.
limitations: All individual link probabilities are themselves uncertain
Positive correlation structure is estimated, not measured
Circuit-breakers may not function in novel configurations
The conjunction fallacy correction itself depends on modeling assumptions
n=0 for the specific US current configuration
